frameworks.name: resource-rational cognition
frameworks.one_line: intelligence as efficient inference under time, energy, and memory constraints

frameworks.name: inductive bias
frameworks.one_line: inference from limited data requires constraining the hypothesis space

frameworks.name: approximation bias
frameworks.one_line: decisions under limited resources require systematic approximations

frameworks.name: Bayesian inference
frameworks.one_line: normative baseline that the resource-bounded brain approximates

core_question: How can a single computational logic explain both the successes and the systematic errors of human cognition?
key_assumptions: the brain is optimized for inference and decision making under data and resource constraints
cognitive errors are not haphazard but inevitable consequences of bounded optimization
intelligence and stupidity share a common computational substrate
key_findings: errors across many cognitive domains follow patterns predicted by inductive- and approximation-bias accounts
framework spans perception, memory, language, reasoning, and decision making
interpretations: human cognition is best understood as resource-bounded optimization rather than as flawed rationality
inductive bias and approximation bias jointly constitute a unifying logic of human intelligence
